revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:兄猾]] {{Infobox person|death_place={{ill|Uda|qid=Q11449912|1=Uda}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|relatives={{ill|Otoukashi|qid=Q55530202|1=Otoukashi}}}}
'''Eukashi''' (兄猾) is a figure from ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} mentioned in sources such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. In the Kojiki, his name is recorded as '''Aniugashi''' (兄宇迦斯). He was the elder brother of [[Otoukashi]] (弟猾). == Biography == Both the ''Kojiki'' (Middle Volume) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) recount a similar story: {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (referred to as Kamuyamato Iwarebiko no Sumeramikoto) struggled to reach {{ill|Uda|en|Uda|qid=Q225843|lt=Uda|1=Uda}}’s Ugachinomura in Yamato Province, guided by the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|qid=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}} and supported by [[Michinoomi|Michinoomi no Mikoto]]. According to the ''Shoki'' chronology, Emperor Jimmu summoned Eukashi and Otoukashi, local leaders of Uda Province, on August 2 in the year戊午 (corresponding to [[:en:663 BCE|663 BCE]]). However, Eukashi refused to come. In the ''Kojiki,'' Yatagarasu approached Eukashi with the following message: {{quotation|“Now, the child of the Heavenly Deities has arrived. Will you serve him?”}}<ref name="Kojiki">''Kojiki,'' Middle Volume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref> Eukashi retaliated by shooting a whistling arrow (鳴鏑, narikabura) at the messenger and driving it away. Instead of facing Jimmu’s forces directly, Eukashi devised a plan: - According to the ''Kojiki,'' he failed to gather an army. - The ''Shoki'' states he observed the overwhelming “imperial forces”<ref name="Shoki">''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Jimmu Pre-Ascension Chronicles,戊午, August 2 entry.</ref> and was too intimidated to fight. Eukashi constructed a new palace with a hidden trap designed to kill Jimmu during a feast. However, his treachery was revealed by his brother, Otoukashi, who had answered Jimmu’s summons. As a result, [[Michinoomi|Michinoomi no Mikoto]] and [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] forced Eukashi to enter the trap first, armed with swords and bows. Eukashi fell victim to his own device and was crushed to death. (In the ''Kojiki,'' he is struck down before being trapped.) The place where Eukashi perished became known as Chihara (血原, "Blood Plain") in Uda. - In the ''Kojiki,'' Eukashi’s body was immediately dismembered after death. - In the ''Shoki,'' his body was dragged out and cut, spilling blood that reached ankle height. After the incident, Otoukashi prepared a celebratory feast with sake and beef. According to the ''Kojiki,'' Emperor Jimmu sang a victory song (the ''Kume no Uta,'' 久米歌): {{quotation|In Uda's high castle, I set snares for the plovers, but the plovers eluded me. Instead, a great whale (hawk) was caught. If the old wife seeks sustenance, give her the sparse fruits of the soba grass. But if the young wife seeks sustenance, give her the plentiful fruits of the sakaki tree. Ee, shakoshiya! This is the sound of resentment and jealousy. Ah, shakoshiya! This is the sound of mocking laughter.}}<ref name="Kojiki"/> The ''Shoki'' identifies this song as part of the ''Kume no Uta'' repertoire. It notes variations in performance styles and gestures used when performed at the Imperial Court's [[Gagaku]] Bureau.<ref name="Shoki"/> The ''Shoki'' continues with Jimmu’s subsequent journey to {{ill|Yoshino|en|Yoshino|qid=Q202111|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino}}, where he encountered the local deities [[Ihikura]], [[Isobiwake]], and [[Niemochi no Ko]] (''Kojiki'' places this encounter before the Eukashi incident). == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Japan's History 1: From Myth to History,'' by [[Mitsuteru Inoue]], [[:en:Chūōkōron-sha|Chūōkōron-sha]], 1965. * ''Special Issue: The Mystery of ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki,'' Historical Mysteries Series 6,'' "The Mysteries and Problems of Ancient Emperors" by [[Toshio Kobayashi]], [[Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha]], 1986.
== See Also == * [[List of People Before the Asuka Period]] * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} * [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] * [[Michinoomi|Michinoomi no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] * [[Utsunomiya Castle False Ceiling Incident]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|兄猾|version=99407351|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:兄猾]]
